title: Dendranthema arcticum
taxonomy: Dendranthema arcticum is classified within the family Asteraceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, such as its inflorescence, leaves, and fruit. Within the Asteraceae family, Dendranthema arcticum is further classified as a member of the tribe Anthemideae, which is a large group of daisy-like plants. The tribe Anthemideae is a subfamily of the Asteraceae family known as Asteroideae, which is one of the two main subfamilies within the family. Further classification of Dendranthema arcticum would involve examining its relationships to other plants within the tribe Anthemideae.
uses: Dendranthema arcticum, commonly known as Arctic Chrysanthemum, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. Its extracts have been studied for their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, suggesting its possible application in treating various diseases. The plant's ability to inhibit the growth of certain bacteria and fungi may contribute to its potential use in wound healing and skin care. Additionally, its antioxidant properties could help protect against oxidative stress and cell damage. While more research is needed to confirm its medicinal uses, Dendranthema arcticum has shown promise in traditional and modern medicine.

intro: Dendranthema arcticum, also known as Arctic Daisy or Arctic Chrysanthemum, is a flowering plant species native to the Arctic tundra regions of Asia and Europe. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, offering various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of colds, fever, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Dendranthema arcticum include flavonoids, sesquiterpenes, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, often in combination with other herbs to enhance its therapeutic effects.
